event: East Polynesian voyagers, likely from the Kermadec Islands or New Zealand, settle Norfolk Island, establishing the earliest known human presence on the island.
The Polynesian settlement is abandoned, leaving behind stone tools, banana cultivation, and Polynesian rat remains as the only traces of these first inhabitants.
Captain James Cook becomes the first European to sight and land on the uninhabited island during his second Pacific voyage, naming it after the Duchess of Norfolk.
Lieutenant Philip Gidley King establishes the first British penal settlement on Norfolk Island just weeks after the First Fleet's arrival at Sydney Cove.
HMS Sirius is wrecked on the reef at Slaughter Bay, stranding supplies and people and deepening the fledgling settlement's hardship.
Britain abandons and largely destroys the first settlement, removing all inhabitants to Van Diemen's Land and leaving the island deserted.
A second penal settlement is founded as a place of extreme punishment for the worst reoffending convicts, gaining a reputation for brutal severity.
Captain Alexander Maconochie introduces his reformist 'marks system' of rehabilitation, briefly transforming conditions in the penal colony.
A failed convict uprising known as the Cooking Pot Mutiny leads to executions and reinforces the island's grim reputation.
The second penal settlement is closed and the convicts are transferred to Tasmania, ending Norfolk Island's era as a prison.
The entire community of Pitcairn Islanders, descendants of the HMS Bounty mutineers and their Tahitian companions, resettle on Norfolk Island.
Some Pitcairn families, homesick for their original home, return to Pitcairn Island, while the majority remain to build a permanent community on Norfolk.
The Melanesian Mission of the Anglican Church establishes its headquarters and St Barnabas chapel at Norfolk Island to train Pacific Islander missionaries.
Bishop John Coleridge Patteson, head of the Melanesian Mission, is killed at Nukapu, leading to later commemorations on the island.
Norfolk Island is formally placed under the administrative authority of the colony of New South Wales.
The federation of the Australian colonies into the Commonwealth of Australia sets the stage for a transfer of responsibility over the island.
Administration of Norfolk Island is transferred from New South Wales to the Commonwealth of Australia under the Norfolk Island Act.
The Melanesian Mission relocates its headquarters from Norfolk Island to Siota in the Solomon Islands, ending its long missionary presence.
An airfield is constructed on the island during World War II, and Norfolk serves as a refuelling and staging point for Allied aircraft in the Pacific.
A New Zealand garrison is stationed on the island to defend the strategically located airfield amid the Pacific theatre of war.
Norfolk Island commemorates the centenary of the arrival of the Pitcairn Islanders, reinforcing its distinctive Bounty-descended cultural identity.
Commercial air services begin operating regularly to the island, boosting the development of tourism as a mainstay of the economy.
The Royal Commission led by Sir John Nimmo examines the island's status and recommends fuller integration with Australia.
The Norfolk Island Act grants the territory limited self-government, establishing a Legislative Assembly responsible for many local affairs.
The first elections under self-government are held, and the new Legislative Assembly begins administering the island's internal affairs.
The Kingston and Arthur's Vale Historic Area, with its Georgian convict-era buildings, is recognised as a site of major heritage significance.
Norfolk Island holds a referendum and continued debate over its political future and degree of autonomy from Australia.
An Australian parliamentary review recommends reforms to address the island's financial sustainability and governance arrangements.
Facing a deepening fiscal crisis, the island signs a funding agreement with the Australian government in exchange for greater oversight and reform commitments.
The Kingston and Arthur's Vale Historic Area is inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List as part of the Australian Convict Sites.
The Australian Parliament passes legislation abolishing Norfolk Island's self-government, ending the Legislative Assembly and extending mainland laws to the territory.
Self-government is formally abolished as Norfolk Island is integrated into the Australian taxation, welfare, immigration, and healthcare systems, with the Regional Council taking over local services.
Residents are brought under Australian Medicare, social security, and income tax for the first time, transforming daily life and the local economy.
The COVID-19 pandemic halts tourism, the island's economic lifeline, prompting border closures and Australian government support measures.
The Australian government dissolves the Norfolk Island Regional Council and appoints an administrator to manage local government affairs.
Ongoing campaigns by residents and advocacy groups press the United Nations and Australia to recognise Norfolk Island's right to self-determination.
